1: Isaiah 23 is the final and culminating burden oracle of Isaiah 13-23: the burden of Tyre (massa Tsor). Tyre was the greatest maritime trading city of the ancient world — a Phoenician city-state on a small island off the modern Lebanese coast (later connected to the mainland by Alexander the Great's causeway, built during his famous 7-month siege of 332 BCE). 'Wail, ships of Tarshish' (yeliloo oniyot tarshish, wail O ships of Tarshish) — Tarshish (probably Tartessos in Spain or Sardinia) was the western terminus of Phoenician trade. The ships of Tarshish were the largest ocean-going vessels of the ancient world (1 Kings 10:22: Solomon's Tarshish fleet brought gold, silver, ivory, apes, and baboons). The news comes from Cyprus (Kittim, the island of Cyprus, where Tyre had important colonies and commercial interests) — Tyre is destroyed.
2: 'Be still, you people of the coastland — you whom Sidon's merchants, who sailed the seas, once made wealthy' — the silence of shock: 'domu yoshevei iy, be silent O inhabitants of the coastland' (domu = be still/silent in stunned grief). Sidon (Tyre's sister city, the older of the two great Phoenician ports, 35 miles north of Tyre on the Lebanese coast) is here associated with Tyre in the trading network. 'Whom the merchants of Sidon, who cross the sea, have made wealthy' (asher socharei Tsidon oberai yam milu'ach, whom the merchants of Sidon who pass over the sea have filled) — the whole coastland's prosperity was built on Phoenician maritime trade. Sidon's own role in the oracle is that of a partner who must mourn Tyre's fall.
3: 'The grain of Shihor — the harvest of the Nile — crossed the great waters and became her income; she was the market town of nations' — the grain trade through Tyre: 'beme'um may, veqatsir yeor tevu'atah, and on great waters the seed of Shihor, the harvest of the Nile, was her revenue.' Shihor (shichor, the turbid one) = the Nile delta branch, or the Wadi el-Arish (the brook of Egypt) — the Egyptian grain supply. Tyre was the broker (sochar goyim, merchant/marketplace of nations) that connected Egypt's agricultural surplus to the Mediterranean world's consumption needs. The grain trade from Egypt through Phoenician shipping to the Mediterranean world is historically documented — Tyre was the critical middleman in one of the ancient world's most important commodity flows.
4: 'Be ashamed, O Sidon, for the sea has spoken — the sea's own fortress declares: I have not travailed or given birth; I have raised no young men and nurtured no young women' — Sidon called to shame: 'boshi Tsidon ki amar yam, be ashamed O Sidon for the sea has said' — the sea itself speaks as a witness. 'Ma'oz hayam, the strength/fortress of the sea' (the sea-fortress = Tyre on its island) says: 'lo chalti velo yiladeti, I have not travailed and I have not given birth; lo gidaleti bahurim, lo rommamti betulot, I have not raised up young men, I have not brought up young women.' The sea-fortress Tyre speaks as a barren mother — the city that should be teeming with life (the young men and women of a flourishing maritime population) declares itself childless. The barrenness metaphor = the cessation of Tyre's commercial generation of wealth and people.
5: 'When the news reaches Egypt, they will writhe with anguish at what has happened to Tyre' — the global reaction to Tyre's fall: 'ka'asher shema lemitsrayim, as when the news is heard in Egypt.' Egypt will writhe (yachilu, will writhe with labor pains = the same labor-pain anguish of 13:8 and 21:3 for the nations at the Day of the LORD) 'at the news of Tyre' (keshama Tsor, when the news about Tyre is heard). Egypt's dependence on Tyre as the broker of Mediterranean trade means that Tyre's fall directly impacts Egypt's economic interests. The pain of Egypt is the pain of a trade partner losing its essential network.
6: 'Sail across to Tarshish! Wail, you people of the coastland!' — the flight command: 'ivru Tarshish, cross over to Tarshish!' — the Phoenician refugees are directed to flee to their western colonial outpost in Spain/Sardinia. 'Heliilu yoshevei iy, wail O inhabitants of the coastland!' — the coastland (the Phoenician coast and its island colonies) called to lament. The irony: Tarshish, which represented the farthest extension of Phoenician commercial power, is now the refuge for Phoenician refugees. The network of commerce becomes the network of escape routes.
7: 'Is this the city that you once celebrated — so ancient in its origins, a city whose people sailed out to settle far-off shores?' — the rhetorical question about Tyre's identity: 'hazot lakhem alizah, is this to you your exultant/joyful city?' The 'exultant city' (alizah, rejoicing/jubilant) reduced to ruin. 'Asher qadmatah le'olam, whose origin is from ancient time' — Tyre claimed enormous antiquity. Herodotus (2.44) records that the Tyrian priests told him the Heracles temple was 2300 years old — placing Tyre's founding around 2750 BCE. 'Takalineha ragleyha merachok lagur, her feet carried her far away to sojourn' — the Phoenicians' colonization: Carthage (814 BCE), Cyprus, Sicily, Sardinia, Spain — 'far-off shores' settled by Tyrian commercial outposts.
8: 'Who planned this against Tyre — the crown-bestowing city, whose traders were princes, whose merchants were among the most celebrated people on earth?' — the rhetorical question about the architect of Tyre's fall: 'mi ya'atz zot al-Tsor ha'atarah, who planned this against Tyre the crowning one?' Tyre as 'the crown-bestowing' (ha'atarah, the one who gives crowns) — Tyre's commercial power enabled it to raise cities to power (planting colonies, establishing trading posts). 'Asher sochareiha sarim, kena'aneiha nikbaddei-erets, whose merchants are princes, whose Canaanites are among the honored of the earth' — the merchants of Tyre hold princely status, and even the ordinary 'Canaanites' (traders — Canaan/Phoenicia being associated with trade to the point where 'Canaanite' became a synonym for 'merchant' in later Hebrew, Proverbs 31:24; Zechariah 14:21) are honored throughout the world.
9: 'The LORD of hosts planned it — to strip the pride from all magnificence and to humiliate all the famous of the earth' — the divine answer to Isaiah 23:8's question: 'YHWH tseva'ot yaazhah, YHWH of hosts has planned it.' The purpose: 'lechalel ge'on kol-tzibi, to profane/strip the pride of all glory/splendor' and 'lehaqel kol-nikbaddei erets, to bring contempt on all the honored/famous of the earth.' The same two purposes as Isaiah 2:11-12 (the Day of the LORD against everything proud and lofty): the Tyre oracle is the specific commercial-power instance of the universal Day-of-the-LORD pattern. YHWH's purpose in judging Tyre is not specific animosity toward Phoenicia but the universal judgment against human pride and the human claim to self-sufficiency.
10: 'Flow through your land like the Nile, O daughter of Tarshish; there is nothing to hold you back any longer' — the liberation of Tarshish from Tyrian control: 'ivri artzekhemayior bat-tarshish, cross/flow through your land like the Nile, O daughter of Tarshish.' With Tyre destroyed, Tarshish (Tyre's western colony) is free: 'ein mozen od, there is no longer a belt/girdle' (mozen = girdle/control) restraining them. Tyre as a colonial power controlled the western Mediterranean trade routes and the daughter-cities' commerce. The liberation of Tarshish from Tyrian commercial hegemony = the freedom of the western Mediterranean from Phoenician dominance. The Nile-flow image suggests abundance flowing freely without restriction.
11: 'The LORD stretched his hand out over the sea and shook the kingdoms; he has issued his order against Phoenicia to demolish its fortresses' — the divine warrior against the sea: 'YHWH natah yado al-hayam, YHWH stretched out his hand over the sea' — the theophanic gesture of cosmic power (the same 'outstretched hand' from the judgment-cycle of Isaiah 9:12-10:4 and Isaiah 14:26-27, now applied to the Mediterranean sea and Tyre's island). 'Hirgiz mamlekhot, he has shaken the kingdoms' — the kingdoms that depended on Tyre's trade network all shaken. 'YHWH commanded against Phoenicia (Canaan) to destroy its fortresses' — the divine decree against the Phoenician commercial network.
12: 'He said, Your days of celebration are finished, O crushed virgin daughter of Sidon. Get up; cross to Cyprus — but even there you will have no rest' — the personified Sidon: 'yeamar lo tosifi od laalos, he said: you will no longer rejoice/celebrate' (the exultant commercial joy of Isaiah 23:7 ended). 'Ata anusah vatulat bat-Tsidon, O oppressed/crushed virgin daughter of Sidon' — Sidon personified as a young woman crushed by military force. The command to flee to Cyprus (Kittim, the closest Mediterranean refuge) is even denied: 'gam-sham lo-yanuach lakh, even there you will have no rest.' Rest (nuach, the resting of security) — the same word as YHWH giving rest from oppression (Isaiah 14:3) — is denied to Sidon in Cyprus. The entire network of Phoenician commercial refuge offers no escape from the divine judgment.
13: 'Look at the land of the Chaldeans — a people who were nothing until Assyria raised them up; they erected siege towers against Tyre, stripped its palaces bare, and turned it to rubble' — the historical instrument of Tyre's judgment: the Chaldeans (Kasdim, the Babylonians under Nebuchadnezzar). 'Hinneh erets Kasdim zeh ha'am lo hayah, behold the land of the Chaldeans — this people was nothing.' The Chaldean rise to power is noted: 'Ashshur yissedah letziyyim, Assyria established/founded it for the jackals/desert-creatures' (or: 'Assyria raised them up') — the Chaldeans (southern Babylonian tribal confederacy) were a minor player until the Neo-Babylonian Empire of Nabopolassar (625-605 BCE) and Nebuchadnezzar II (605-562 BCE). Nebuchadnezzar's 13-year siege of Tyre (585-572 BCE) partially fulfilled this oracle, though Alexander's 332 BCE destruction was more complete.
14: 'Wail, ships of Tarshish — your fortress harbour is destroyed!' — the inclusion: the oracle comes full circle from Isaiah 23:1's 'wail, ships of Tarshish' to this closing repetition. The ships of Tarshish called to wail at the opening and closing of the oracle — a literary frame. 'Yeliloo oniyot tarshish ki shudad ma'oz khem, wail O ships of Tarshish for your fortress is destroyed.' The 'fortress' (ma'oz, stronghold) = Tyre the island-fortress that protected and enabled the commercial network. Without the fortress-port of Tyre, the Tarshish ships have no safe harbor, no commercial center, no protection network.
15: 'In that day Tyre will be forgotten for seventy years — the span of one king's lifetime. At the end of seventy years, Tyre will be treated like the woman in the prostitute's song' — the seventy-year framework: 'vehayah bayom hahu venishshechah Tsor shiv'im shanah kimei melech echad, and it will be in that day that Tyre will be forgotten seventy years like the days of one king.' The 'seventy years' is also the framework of the Babylonian exile (Jeremiah 25:11-12; Isaiah 29:10): 'the whole country will become a desolate wasteland, and these nations will serve the king of Babylon seventy years.' The parallel between Tyre's seventy-year eclipse and Judah's seventy-year exile suggests both are under the same divine governance of historical time. After seventy years: Tyre 'will be treated like the prostitute's song' (kehashirah hazzonah, like the song of the prostitute).
16: 'Pick up your harp, walk the streets, O forgotten prostitute; play your sweetest tune and sing song after song so that someone might remember you' — the prostitute's song: 'kichi kinnor, sivi ir, zonah nishkachah, take a harp, go around the city, O forgotten prostitute!' The harp-carrying street-musician as a metaphor for Tyre's commercial re-engagement after eclipse: using cultural attractions to regain commercial partners. 'Heytivi naggein, harbi shir lema'an tizzakeri, play well, multiply your songs so that you will be remembered.' Tyre's commercial 'songs' = the commercial attractions (goods, services, trade relationships) that she uses to re-establish her network after seventy years. The prostitute metaphor for commercial trading ('selling herself to all the kingdoms') is extended in Ezek 27-28 (Tyre as the 'perfect beauty,' then the guilty merchant of the sanctuary).
17: 'At the end of seventy years the LORD will attend to Tyre, and she will return to her trading — selling herself to all the kingdoms of the world' — the restoration after seventy years: 'vehayah miqets shiv'im shanah yipqod YHWH et-Tsor, and it will be at the end of seventy years that YHWH will attend to/visit Tyre.' The paqad (attend to/visit) is the covenant-activation verb — YHWH's visiting produces either judgment or restoration depending on context (Exodus 3:16: YHWH visited Israel in Egypt; 2 Kgs 23:24 negative). Tyre's restoration: 'veshavah el-etnanah, and she will return to her prostitute's wages' (etnan, the wages of a prostitute — the commercial revenue of trading). 'Vezantah et-kol-mamlakhot ha'arets al-penei ha'adamah, and she will prostitute herself to all the kingdoms of the earth on the face of the ground' — universal commercial re-engagement.
18: 'But her merchandise and her earnings will be set apart as holy to the LORD; they will not be stored up or hoarded, for her profit will supply those who live before the LORD with abundant food and quality clothing' — the eschatological consecration of Tyre's commercial wealth: 'vehayah socharah ve'etnanah qodesh laYHWH, and her merchandise and her wages will be holy to YHWH.' The ultimate transformation: Tyre's commercial revenues (the 'prostitute's wages' of Isaiah 23:17) become holy (qodesh, set apart for YHWH). 'It will not be stored up or hoarded' (lo ye'apher velo yechassen, not treasured and not stored as reserve) — the wealth will not be accumulated for Tyre's own power but immediately channeled: 'ki liyoshevim lifnei YHWH yihyeh sacharah, for those who dwell before YHWH will be her merchandise' — supplying the covenant community with 'abundant food and quality clothing.' The final vision: Tyre's commercial power consecrated to sustaining YHWH's people — the commercial economy redeemed and redirected to covenant purposes.
